schools.summary: The Gloss: after rebuking the covetous scribes, Christ commends a widow's giving; Bede on the treasury (gazophylacium) — Christ observes those who bring gifts as he overthrew the traders, praising the worthy and condemning the bad; Christ weighs the gift by the giver's heart, not the amount.

Clarke: 'miserably poor' (her poverty heightening the merit), two mites (lepta) the smallest coins; Gill: Christ took particular notice of her above all the rich — the poor and the widow his care, their mean services in faith and love preferred above the greater gifts of others.

Ellicott: Luke's 'Of a truth' for Mark's 'Amen' (characteristic); Barnes: a gift accepted according to what one has (2 Corinthians 8:12); the widow cast in 'more than they all' — God measures by the heart and the proportion to the giver's means, not the amount; the least given in love the most in his sight.

Gill: the rich gave of their surplus and retained much; Robertson: she cast in 'all her living' (bion, her livelihood) — the decisive contrast; the gift measured by what it costs the giver, the widow giving all in faith (trusting God to provide), the rich a fraction trusting still in their wealth — sacrifice and love the true measure.

themes: all the living that she had
the gift measured by its cost

schools.summary: Eusebius: the temple's beauty, yet doomed for the presumption of its worshippers; Bede: ordained to be overthrown lest new believers be carried away by the rites and beauties; Ambrose: nothing made with hands endures (age, violence, fire) — the vanity of earthly splendour and the providence removing what hinders faith.

Robertson: the prediction a shock to the disciples after the triumphal entry; Clarke: literally fulfilled in the Roman destruction (AD 70); Christ foretells total demolition (not one stone upon another), verified to the letter — the certainty and exactness of his word of judgment, the text of the whole discourse.

Cyril of Jerusalem: the balance Christ teaches — not curious about the time (Acts 1:7), nor slumbering, but watching; he gives the signs lest they be deceived by Antichrist; the twofold question (when, and what sign) framing the discourse, which speaks of both Jerusalem's near judgment and the final coming.

Ellicott: 'Christ' an interpolation, the bare 'I am' usurping the divine/Messianic claim; Clarke: many false Christs arose before Jerusalem's fall (cf. Matthew 24:5); Christ's first counsel is vigilance against deception — beware the impostors who exploit the hope of the kingdom, and follow them not.

themes: take heed that ye be not deceived
many shall come in my name

schools.summary: Gregory: God foretells the woes that they may disturb less when foreseen ('darts foreseen wound the less'); 'wars' = enemies without, 'commotions' = strife within; the last tribulation preceded by many — be not terrified, for these must come first but are not yet the end; composure, not panic, amid the upheavals.

themes: the end is not by and by

schools.summary: Gill (cf. Matthew 24:7); Barnes: God sets kingdom against kingdom in judgment (Haggai 2:22); Christ foretells widespread war (nation against nation) — fulfilled in the convulsions before Jerusalem's fall and recurrently before the end; the wars foreknown and foretold, within God's providence, to be discerned without alarm.

Ellicott: 'pestilences' peculiar to Luke (the physician's eye), the famine/pestilence wordplay (limos/loimos); Robertson: 'fearful sights' (phobēthra) only here; the signs extend to nature — earthquakes, famines, pestilences, heavenly portents — foretold and foreknown, to be discerned without terror.

Gregory: the disturbance of men's hearts (persecution) before the elements, that the world's calamity be seen as retribution; Cyril: fulfilled before Jerusalem's fall — the apostles imprisoned, Paul before Festus and Agrippa and sent to Caesar; persecution the disciples' appointed lot, foretold, suffered 'for my name's sake.'

themes: they shall lay their hands on you

schools.summary: Gill: the persecution turned to a threefold testimony — opportunity to witness before kings, confirmation of the disciples' faith, and a testimony against the persecutors; Cyril: 'for the glory of martyrdom' — suffering turned to honour; God overruling the enemies' malice to the spread of the gospel.

themes: it shall turn to you for a testimony

schools.summary: Robertson: 'not to meditate beforehand' (promeletan, conning a speech), Ellicott: less anxiety than Mark's phrase; Barnes: a settled principle to depend on God in all trials; not forbidding diligence generally but the anxious rehearsing of a defence — trust God, not self-preparation, in the crisis of persecution (cf. Luke 12:11).

themes: not to meditate before what ye shall answer

schools.summary: Clarke/Ellicott: the twofold gift — 'a mouth' (ready utterance) and 'wisdom' (knowing what to say), both Christ's gift as promised Moses (Exodus 4:15-16); irresistible, the adversaries unable to gainsay or resist (cf. Stephen, Acts 6:10); the ground of freedom from anxious forethought — Christ himself the disciple's mouth and wisdom.

themes: I will give you a mouth and wisdom

schools.summary: Barnes: betrayal by one's own household fulfilling Micah 7:6, and martyrdom of some (Stephen, James — Acts 7:59; 12:2); Robertson: 'cause to be put to death' (causative); the persecution reaching the closest ties and to death, all foreknown — Christ forewarning the full cost of discipleship that they not be shaken.

themes: some shall they put to death

schools.summary: Barnes: the world hates the disciples as it hated Christ (John 17:14) — hated of all men 'for my name's sake,' not for any fault but for belonging to Christ; the hatred an honour and a fellowship in his sufferings (cf. Luke 6:22), foretold that the disciple not be surprised, yet bounded by God's care.

Gill: not entire bodily safety (since martyrdom was foretold) but that the disciple is no loser — all things work for good, death itself an advantage; Robertson: spiritual safety, Paul's confidence that to die is gain (Philippians 1:21); 'not a hair shall perish' = the soul and eternal welfare untouchable, the worst men can do but send him to glory.

debates.question: How can 'not a hair shall perish' stand with 'some shall be put to death'?

sides.position: It means the soul's safety and eternal welfare, not entire bodily preservation — the martyr no loser

themes: not a hair of your head perish

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: 'in your patience possess your souls' — by patiently bearing affliction, keep your inward peace and self-possession (the peace the world cannot take), or (future reading) by perseverance unto the end be saved (Matthew 24:13); patience the grace by which the disciple holds fast his soul, its present peace and eternal safety, through persecution.

debates.question: Does 'possess your souls' mean keep your inward peace or save your lives/souls finally?

sides.position: Keep your self-possession and inward peace amid affliction
By perseverance unto the end be finally saved

themes: in your patience possess your souls

schools.summary: Bede: the discourse turns to the actual desolation by the Roman army; Eusebius: a final desolation (unlike the Babylonian, no restoration); Augustine: Daniel's 'abomination of desolation'; Luke gives the concrete sign (the encircling armies) for the Gentile reader — a recognizable warning that the watchful might flee and escape.

Ellicott: 'the countries' = the open fields; the threefold command to flee (to the mountains, out of the city, not into it) reversing the instinct to seek the city's safety — fulfilled in the Christians' flight to Pella, escaping the siege; God gives warning and direction, the watchful delivered, the heedless destroyed.

Gill: the days of God's vengeance on the nation for rejecting and crucifying the Messiah; Barnes: fulfilling the prophets' long-threatened judgment (Deuteronomy 28; Daniel 9:26-27); the fall not arbitrary but the just recompense of sin, 'all things written' fulfilled — God's judgments sure, the prophetic word vindicated, privilege abused bringing the heavier doom.

Ellicott: Luke's distinctive word for 'distress' (great necessity/straitness, used by Paul); Barnes: the famine-horrors of the siege (Lamentations 4:10); a special woe on mothers least able to flee, 'wrath upon this people' — the judicial visitation dreadful in severity, sparing not the helpless, foretold with Christ's pity.

Gill records the toll (1,100,000 perished); the threefold doom — slaughter, captivity among the nations, the city trodden down by Gentiles; Lightfoot cautiously doubts whether 'until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled' points to a restoration or completes before the end — the phrase marking a bounded term, handled with reserve.

debates.question: What is meant by 'until the times of the Gentiles be fulfilled'?

sides.position: Christ speaks of the fall, doubtful whether of any restoration; the limit's meaning veiled
The period of the Gentiles' ingathering, after which Israel's fulness (cf. Romans 11:25)

themes: fall by the edge of the sword

schools.summary: Bede/Ambrose set the cosmic signs after the times of the Gentiles, of the final coming; Eusebius/Chrysostom: the sun, moon, and stars darkened because eclipsed by the greater glory of Christ the true Sun (as stars fade at sunrise); the nations in distress and perplexity, the seas roaring — all creation shaken before the day of the Lord.

themes: signs in the sun, moon, and stars

schools.summary: Barnes: 'hearts failing' = fainting, ready to die with terror at the coming calamities; Clarke: men fainting away through fear; 'the powers of heaven shaken' — the celestial order convulsed, all stability dissolving; if such terror at Judea's judgment, how much more at the final day — the coming a day of fainting dread for the unprepared.

themes: the terror of the last days

schools.summary: Robertson: the coming 'not till then,' not of immediate realization, the time purposely vague; Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the testimonies of the Son's coming in glory; the marks — visible ('they shall see'), in the cloud of glory (cf. Acts 1:9), with power and great glory — the King and Judge, the opposite of his lowly first coming.

themes: the Son of man coming in a cloud

schools.summary: Gregory: Christ turns from the reprobate's dread to the elect's consolation — 'lift up your heads' = raise the mind to the joys of the heavenly country, rejoicing that the world (whose friends they are not) is closing and redemption is near; the signs that terrify the world are the believer's hope, the coming his full redemption.

themes: look up and lift up your heads

schools.summary: Henry: the disciples to observe the signs of the times as surely as the budding trees announce summer; as in nature a chain of causes, so in providence one event follows another — the signs a reliable index of the nearness of the coming; Luke's 'all the trees' generalizes the fig-tree figure; spiritual discernment, not date-setting, commended.

themes: reading the signs of the times

schools.summary: Robertson: 'shoot forth,' the word 'summer' (theros) not harvest; Wesley: 'ye know of yourselves' — though none teach you, the sign self-evident; as the budding trees plainly announce summer without instruction, so the foretold signs are plain tokens to the watchful — the signs legible, the fault in those who will not read them (cf. Luke 12:56).

Clarke: 'the kingdom of God nigh' = the gospel prevailing everywhere after the Jewish state's fall; Robertson: the consummation of the kingdom, not its beginning; Ellicott: Luke expands the simpler 'near, even at the doors'; the signs announcing both the kingdom's wider advance and its final consummation — read by the watchful as heralds of the kingdom.

debates.question: What 'kingdom of God' is meant as nigh?

sides.position: The gospel's wider advance after Jerusalem's fall
The final consummation of the kingdom at the coming

themes: the kingdom of God is nigh

schools.summary: Robertson surveys the crux: some apply it to the whole discourse (and charge Jesus with error or misreporting), but the better reading refers it to the destruction of Jerusalem, fulfilled in AD 70 within that generation — vindicating Christ's word; Clarke: 'this generation' = the men then living, or the Jewish race surviving till all is fulfilled; the time-limit attaches to the near fulfilment, the final coming left vague.

debates.question: What does 'this generation shall not pass till all be fulfilled' refer to?

sides.position: The destruction of Jerusalem (AD 70), fulfilled within that generation
'This generation' = the Jewish race, surviving till all is fulfilled
The whole discourse including the second coming (forcing a charge of error)

schools.summary: Robertson: the emphatic 'shall not pass away,' placed just after the difficult v.32 to stake the permanence of his word on the prophecy's truth; Barnes: the abiding word of God (Isaiah 40:8); Cyril of Jerusalem: the world passes for a fairer to be manifest (Isaiah 34:4); Christ's words more enduring than heaven and earth — a claim of deity, the sure ground for faith.

themes: heaven and earth shall pass away
my words shall not pass away

schools.summary: Theophylact: the remedy is watching and prayer; Basil: 'take heed to yourselves' = keep an ever-watchful guard on the soul, fleeing sin by reason as the brutes shun deadly food; the threefold danger — surfeiting, drunkenness, and the cares of this life — overcharging the heart and leaving it unprepared, so the day comes unawares.

themes: hearts overcharged with surfeiting and cares

schools.summary: Gill: 'as a snare' — sudden, unavoidable, universal, like a bird caught and unable to escape; Barnes: the snare denotes suddenness, sprung when unexpected (1 Thessalonians 5:2); Clarke: 'the whole earth' = the land of Judea; the day comes on all the careless — the suddenness the very reason for the watchfulness charged.

themes: as a snare shall it come
all that dwell on the earth

schools.summary: Gill: watch against every sin and snare, pray continually for fresh grace; Barnes: 'to stand before the Son of man' = approbation, acquittal, accepted at his coming while others are condemned (Psalm 1:5; Revelation 6:17); the twofold duty — watching and praying together the safeguard against the snare, the way to escape the woes and be found ready.

themes: watch ye therefore and pray always
to stand before the Son of man

schools.summary: Bede: Christ confirms his command (watch and pray) by his example — as the Passion drew near, instant in teaching, watching, and prayer; teaching by day in the temple, retiring to Olivet (the mount of prayer) by night; Theophylact: the evangelists record but a taste of his abundant teaching — Christ's diligence the pattern for his people.

themes: teaching in the temple by day
abode in the mount of Olives

schools.summary: Gill: 'all the people' a large number, some from curiosity, others from love, rising early to hear Christ; Ellicott: the verb 'came early' used in the OT for rising early to seek God (Job 8:5; Jeremiah 25:3) — the people's eager devotion to Christ's precious word, contrasting the rulers' plot, preserving him for his hour.
